interactions.summary: Jiang defines Asha through Kantian moral law, free will, humans as ends, monotheism, and Plato's cave as a model of escaping and returning with truth.

speaker_notes.note: All focus refs are Jiang monologue with philosophical comparison and metaphor.

claims.claim: Asha is not merely telling factual truth; it is a system of virtue that makes the person a representative of Ahura Mazda on earth.
Jiang maps Asha onto three Kantian principles: universality, free will, and treating each human as an end rather than a means.
Asha introduces the individual, free choice, and inner truth as concepts that Jiang says revolutionize human history and structure monotheism.
Plato's cave becomes Jiang's metaphor for Asha: liberation is incomplete until the freed person returns to help others escape.

signature_moments.moment: Every action is imagined as immediately echoed by the whole universe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: everything you do will be reflected throughout the universe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This turns morality into cosmic resonance rather than social rule-following.

signature_moments.moment: Every single human life is as valuable as all human life together.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Every life, every human life is as valuable as all human life together.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang a hard anti-sacrificial rule against utopian violence.

signature_moments.moment: Finding the sun is not enough; the truth demands a descent back into the cave.
signature_moments.source_phrase: for me to fully achieve Asha, I must go down and tell them the truth
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It transforms enlightenment from private escape into public obligation.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Kant's highest moral law, used by Jiang as a bridge for explaining Asha through universality, free will, and humans as ends.
The rule that no person may be sacrificed as a means to another political or moral goal.
Plato's cave is read as an Asha model: escape illusion, see truth, then return to free others.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang states here that Zoroastrianism creates the structure for Judaism, Christianity, and Islam; later source summaries should preserve the 2025-11-14 date when comparing his monotheism claims.
